elements.sent-2411-001: Correlated observations are ubiquitous phenomena in a plethora of scientific avenues.
elements.sent-2411-002: Tackling this dependence among test statistics has been one of the pertinent problems in simultaneous inference.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, very little literature exists that elucidates the effect of correlation on different testing procedures under general distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we address this gap in a unified way by considering the multiple testing problem under a general correlated framework.
elements.sent-2411-005: We establish an upper bound on the family-wise error rate(FWER) of Bonferroni's procedure for equicorrelated test statistics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Consequently, we find that for a quite general class of distributions, Bonferroni FWER asymptotically tends to zero when the number of hypotheses approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-007: We extend this result to general positively correlated elliptically contoured setups.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also present examples of distributions for which Bonferroni FWER has a strictly positive limit under equicorrelation.
elements.sent-2411-009: We extend the limiting zero results to the class of step-down procedures under quite general correlated setups.
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, the probability of rejecting at least one hypothesis approaches zero asymptotically for any step-down procedure.
elements.sent-2411-011: The results obtained in this work generalize existing results for correlated Normal test statistics and facilitate new insights into the performances of multiple testing procedures under dependence.
elements.sent-2411-012: Familywise error rate, Multiple testing under dependence, Stepwise Procedures, Holm's Method, Elliptically Contoured Distributions.. MSC 2020 Classification.
elements.sent-2411-013: Dependence among observations is a natural phenomenon in a plethora of scientific avenues.
elements.sent-2411-014: It is more so when one is interested in asking several related questions.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, capturing and tackling the correlation among the test statistics corresponding to the various hypotheses is a problem of pivotal importance in simultaneous inference \citep{Chi2024, Gasparin2024, Sarkar2023, Xu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Hence, the research on how correlation among test statistics affects the performances of multiple testing procedures has garnered much interest lately.
elements.sent-2411-017: The equicorrelation structure portrays the simplest kind of dependence.
elements.sent-2411-018: Although simple in form, the equicorrelated setup arises in several applications, e.g., in the problem of comparing a control against several treatments.
elements.sent-2411-019: Consequently, numerous recent works in multiple testing consider the equicorrelated setup \citep{Delattre, Proschan, royspl .}
elements.sent-2411-020: We note that equicorrelated normal test statistics with common mean and variance are exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-021: As also noted by \cite{Gasparin2024 , exchangeability can be induced under any configuration by processing the (potentially non-exchangeable) sequence of test statistics in a uniformly random order.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Hence, if randomization is allowed, it is always possible to satisfy the exchangeability assumption even if the starting sequence has an arbitrary dependence.
elements.sent-2411-023: This result, in turn, gives that the Bonferroni FWER is bounded above by $\alpha(1-\rho)$, $\alpha$ being the desired level.
elements.sent-2411-024: To improve this result, \cite{deybhandari have elucidated that the Bonferroni FWER asymptotically goes to zero for any strictly positive $\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In a related but different direction, \cite{deycstm has obtained upper bounds on the Bonferroni FWER in the equicorrelated setup with small and moderate dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the bounds presented in \cite{deycstm do not converge to zero as the number of hypotheses becomes arbitrarily large.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Hence, there is a scope to find upper bounds on Bonferroni FWER that approaches zero as the number of hypotheses diverges to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-028: Also, all the works mentioned earlier consider equicorrelated normal distributions.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work, we address this gap unifyingly by considering the multiple testing problem under a general correlated framework.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: We first formally introduce the framework with the assumptions and summarize some results on the limiting behavior of the Bonferroni method under the equicorrelated normal setup in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sec:3 studies in detail the limiting behaviors of the Bonferroni FWER under general distributional assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Section \ref{sec:4 extends these results to general positively correlated elliptically contoured setups.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We extend the limiting zero results to the class of step-down FWER-controlling procedures in Section \ref{sec:5 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: A detailed empirical study is presented in Section \ref{sec:6 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We sketch out our contributions and briefly discuss potential problems in similar avenues in Section \ref{sec:7 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Throughout this paper, $H(\mu, \sigma^2)$ represents a cdf $H$ parametrized by the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
elements.subsection-2411-001: The General Equicorrelated Sequence Model Framework
elements.sent-2411-038: We consider a sequence model framework \citep{Finner2001a, FDR2007, Proschan .}
elements.sent-2411-039: We have $n$ (possibly correlated) observations from a distribution $F^{\star}$: \begin{equation}X_{i} {\sim} F^{\star}(\mu_{i},1), \quad i \in \{1, \ldots,n\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: We take unit variances since the literature on the asymptotic multiple testing theory usually considers known variances \citep{Abramovich, Bogdan, Donoho .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Here the $X_{i}$’s are equicorrelated: for some $\rho \in (0,1)$, we have \begin{equation}X_{i} = \sqrt{1-\rho}Z_{i} + \sqrt{\rho} U, \quad i \in \{1, \ldots,n\},\end{equation} where $Z_{i \sim F(\frac{\mu_{i {\sqrt{1-\rho ,1)$ and $U \sim G(0,1)$ independently.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Also, $Z_{i}$’s are independent to each other.
elements.sent-2411-044: These kind of equicorrelated setups are routinely considered in multiple testing literature \citep{Delattre, DickhausThesis, Proschan, royspl .}
elements.sent-2411-045: The equicorrelated setup also includes the problem of comparing several treatments against a control.
elements.sent-2411-046: This paper considers the following multiple testing problem: \begin{equation}H_{0i}:\mu_{i}=0 \quad vs \quad H_{1i}:\mu_{i}>0, \quad 1 \leq i \leq n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: Under the global null $ H_{0 :=\bigcap_{i=1 ^{n H_{0 i}$, each $\mu_{i}$ is zero.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Let $\mathcal{A}$ denote the set of indices corresponding to the true nulls.
elements.sent-2411-049: So, under the global null, $\mathcal{A}$ is $\{1, \ldots, n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Throughout this work, $f^{\star , f, g$ denote the density functions of $F^{\star , F, G$ respectively (their existence is assumed).}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Also, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ denotes the desired level of FWER control.
elements.sent-2411-052: Similar to \cite{deystpa , let $R_{n (T)$ and $V_n(T)$ respectively denote the number of rejections and the number of type I errors of a multiple testing procedure (MTP henceforth) $T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: So, $S_n(T)= R_n(T)-V_n(T)$ is the number of correct rejections.
elements.sent-2411-054: The FWER of procedure $T$ is given by \begin{equation}FWER_{T}(n, \alpha, \Sigma_n)= \mathbb{P}_{\Sigma_{n}}(V_{n}(T)\geq 1) \label{defFWER}\end{equation} where $\Sigma_{n}$ is the covariance matrix of $(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: In many of our results, we shall consider the probability in the r.h.s of \eqref{defFWER under the global null $H_{0}$ at first (and view that as the definition of FWER) for the technical simplicity.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Then we generalize the results obtained in the global null case to arbitrary combination of true and false null hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-057: There are several notions of power in simultaneous statistical inference \citep{Dudoit .}
elements.sent-2411-058: One of these is AnyPwr \citep{Dudoit , which is the probability of rejecting at least one false null.}
elements.sent-2411-060: As in \cite{deystpa , throughout this work, $\Gamma_n$ denotes the $n \times n$ matrix with each diagonal entry equal to 1 and each off-diagonal entry equal to $\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Also, $\Sigma_{n}$ is the $n \times n$ correlation matrix with $(i,j)$'th entry $\rho_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The Bonferroni procedure has been a cornerstone of simultaneous statistical inference.
elements.sent-2411-063: In one-sided settings (as ours), it rejects $H_{0i}$ if $X_{i >(F^{\star )^{-1 (1-\alpha/n) (=c_{Bon , \text{say )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The Bonferroni FWER (when the test statistics have covariance $\Sigma_n$) is given by \begin{equation}FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \Sigma_n) =\mathbb{P}_{\Sigma_n}\left(X_{i}>c_{Bon}\right. \text{for some} \left. i \in \mathcal{A}\right) =\mathbb{P}_{\Sigma_n}\bigg(\bigcup_{i \in \mathcal{A}}\{X_i > c_{Bon}\}\bigg).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \Sigma_n) =P_{\Sigma_n}\left(X_{i}>c_{Bon}\right. for some \left. i \in A\right) =P_{\Sigma_n}\bigg(\bigcup_{i \in A}\{X_i > c_{Bon}\}\bigg).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: Given any $\alpha \in (0,1)$ and $\rho \in [0,1]$, $FWER_{Bon (n, \alpha, \rho)$ is asymptotically bounded above by $\alpha(1-\rho)$ under the global null.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Given any $\alpha \in (0,1)$ and $\rho \in (0,1]$, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty} FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) = 0\end{equation} under any configuration of true and false null hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-068: They extend Theorem \ref{thm2.2 to general correlated normal setups:.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Then, for any $\alpha \in (0,1)$, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty}FWER_{Bon}(n,\alpha,\Sigma_{n}) = 0\end{equation} under any configuration of true and false null hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, those upper bounds do not converge to zero as the number of hypotheses grows to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-071: Also, each of the works and results mentioned in this subsection assumes multivariate normality as the joint distribution of the test statistics.
elements.sent-2411-072: Hence, studying the corresponding limiting behaviors in general equicorrelated structures becomes interesting.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the subsequent sections, we address this gap unifyingly by considering a general equicorrelated setup.
elements.section-2411-003: Limiting Bonferroni FWER under General Equicorrelation \label{sec:3}
elements.sent-2411-074: Firstly we obtain an upper bound on Bonferroni FWER under the general equicorrelated setup.
elements.sent-2411-075: Consider the equicorrelated setup mentioned in Section 2.1 with $\alpha \in (0,1), \rho \in (0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-076: We have, for each $d \in (0,1)$, \begin{equation}FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) \leqslant 1 - G(0) \cdot F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)-\left[G\left(c_{Bon} \cdot \frac{1-d}{\sqrt{\rho}}\right)-G(0)\right] \cdot F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon} \cdot d}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)\end{equation} under the global null hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-077: Under some extra conditions on $f$ and $g$, we prove $\displaystyle \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon \cdot d {\sqrt{1-\rho }\right)=1$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-079: Consider the equicorrelated setup mentioned in Section 2.1 with $\alpha \in (0,1), \rho \in (0,1)$.. We have, \begin{equation}\log \left[\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)\right] = -\alpha \cdot\left[\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)}{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u}\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: We wish to find $\displaystyle \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty P_{n}$.. Now, \begin{equation}\log \left[\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} P_{n}\right] =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\log P_{n}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \big[n \cdot \log \left(F\left(c_{Bon} \cdot t\right)\right)\big] =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\log \left(F\left(c_{Bon}\cdot t\right)\right)}{\frac{1}{n}} =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{h_{1}(n)}{h_{2}(n)} \quad (say) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{h_{1}^{\prime}(n)}{h_{2}^{\prime}(n)} \quad \left(\text{since $h_{1},h_{2} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{F\left(t c_{Bon}\right)} \cdot f\left(t c_{Bon}\right) \cdot t \cdot \frac{d}{d n}\left(c_{Bon}\right)}{-\frac{1}{n^{2}}} =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}-n^{2} t \cdot \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{F\left(t c_{Bon}\right)} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{n^{2} \cdot f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)} \quad \left(\text{since $f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)\cdot \frac{d}{d n}\left(c_{Bon}\right) =\alpha/n^2$}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}-\alpha t \cdot \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)} =-\alpha t \cdot \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\log \left[\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} P_{n}\right] =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\log P_{n}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \big[n \cdot \log \left(F\left(c_{Bon} \cdot t\right)\right)\big] =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\log \left(F\left(c_{Bon}\cdot t\right)\right)}{\frac{1}{n}} =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{h_{1}(n)}{h_{2}(n)} \quad (say) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{h_{1}^{\prime}(n)}{h_{2}^{\prime}(n)} \quad \left(\text{since $h_{1},h_{2} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{F\left(t c_{Bon}\right)} \cdot f\left(t c_{Bon}\right) \cdot t \cdot \frac{d}{d n}\left(c_{Bon}\right)}{-\frac{1}{n^{2}}} =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}-n^{2} t \cdot \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{F\left(t c_{Bon}\right)} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{n^{2} \cdot f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)} \quad \left(\text{since $f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)\cdot \frac{d}{d n}\left(c_{Bon}\right) =\alpha/n^2$}\right) =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}-\alpha t \cdot \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)} =-\alpha t \cdot \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f\left(t c_{Bon}\right)}{f^{*}\left(c_{Bon}\right)}.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-095: Under any configuration of true and false null hypotheses, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{n \rightarrow 0} FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) = 0\end{equation} if the following two conditions are satisfied: (i) There exists positive constant $a$ such that $G(a)<1$.
elements.sent-2411-097: Proceeding along the similar lines as in the proof of \autoref{thm3.1new , we have \begin{equation} FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) =1 -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u =1 -\int_{0}^{\infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u-\int_{-\infty}^{0} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u \geq 1 - F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) \cdot[1-G(0)]- G(0) = \bigg[1-F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)\bigg]\cdot [1-G(0)]. \end{equation} Hence, if we consider only those distributions $G$ for which $G(0)<1$, then, $\displaystyle \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon {\sqrt{1-\rho }\right)<1$ implies $\displaystyle \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty FWER_{Bon (n, \alpha, \rho)>0$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation} FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) =1 -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u =1 -\int_{0}^{\infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u-\int_{-\infty}^{0} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}-\sqrt{\rho} u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) g(u) d u \geq 1 - F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right) \cdot[1-G(0)]- G(0) = \bigg[1-F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)\bigg]\cdot [1-G(0)]. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: Therefore, our present goal (in this subsection) is to find distributions $F$ for which \begin{equation}\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-p}}\right)<1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-099: Towards this, we shall utilize Cantelli's Inequality.
elements.sent-2411-100: This states that for any real-valued random variable $X$ with finite variance $\sigma^{2}$, \begin{equation}P[X\geqslant \lambda + E(X)] \leqslant \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\sigma^{2}+\lambda^{2}} \quad \forall \lambda>0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-101: We apply the above inequality on the test statistic $X_{i :=\sqrt{1-\rho Z_{i +\sqrt{\rho U$ with $\lambda=\sqrt{n/\alpha}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Using Cantelli's inequality, we obtain \begin{equation} P[X_i \geqslant \sqrt{n/\alpha}+\mu_i] \leqslant \frac{\alpha}{n} \implies P[X_i \leqslant \sqrt{n/\alpha}+\mu_i] \geqslant 1-\frac{\alpha}{n} \implies F^{\star}\left(\sqrt{n/\alpha}+\mu_i\right) \geqslant 1-\frac{\alpha}{n} \implies \sqrt{n/\alpha}+\mu_i \geqslant c_{Bon} \implies \sqrt{n/\gamma} \geqslant c_{Bon} \quad (for some $\gamma \in (0,\alpha)$). \end{equation} Therefore, \begin{equation} FWER_{Bon}(n, \alpha, \rho) \geq \bigg[1-F^{n}\left(\frac{c_{Bon}}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)\bigg]\cdot [1-G(0)] \geq \bigg[1-F^{n}\left(\sqrt{\frac{n}{\gamma(1-\rho)}}\right)\bigg]\cdot [1-G(0)]. \end{equation} Hence, it is enough to find a cdf $F$ for which $\displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty F^{n}\left(\sqrt{\frac{n {\gamma(1-\rho) }\right) < 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Towards this, we consider the density of the Pareto distribution: \begin{equation}f_{Z}(z)=\left\{{cl} \frac{b \eta^{b}}{z^{b+1}} z \geqslant \eta 0 else. \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The cdf of $Z$ is given by \begin{equation} F_{Z}(z)=\left\{{cc} 1-\eta^{b} \cdot z^{-b} z \geq \eta 0 else. \right. \end{equation} This implies \begin{equation} F^{n}\left(\sqrt{\frac{n}{\gamma(1-\rho)}}\right) =\left(1-\frac{d}{n^{1+\frac{\delta}{2}}}\right)^{n} \quad (\text{here $d:=\eta^{1+\delta/2}$ is a constant}) \geqslant\left(1-\frac{d}{n^{1+\frac{\delta}{2}}}\right)^{n^{1+\frac{\delta}{2}}} \displaystyle \underset{n \to \infty}{\longrightarrow} e^{-d}. \end{equation} Hence, in this case, the limiting Bonferroni FWER is strictly positive.}
elements.sent-2411-107: We may summarize the findings of this subsection through the following result:.

elements.section-2411-004: Limiting Bonferroni FWER under General Positive Correlation\label{sec:4}
elements.sent-2411-109: A plethora of scientific avenues involves variables with complex correlation structures.
elements.sent-2411-110: This section makes an attempt to generalize the theoretical limiting zero results for the Bonferroni FWER under those circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-111: Towards this, we consider the same sequence model setup mentioned in Section 2.1, but now $\operatorname{\mathbb{C orr}\left(X_{i , X_{j}\right)=\rho_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$ where $\rho_{ij \in (0,1]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Let $\Sigma_{n}$ be the correlation matrix of $X_{1 , \ldots, X_{n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-113: We also assume that the joint density $h(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ is elliptically contoured \citep{SomeshD , i.e., it is given by \begin{equation}h(x) = |\Sigma_n|^{-1 / 2} t\left(x^{\prime} {\Sigma_{n}}^{-1} x\right)\end{equation} where $\int_0^{\infty r^{n-1 t\left(r^2\right) d r<\infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: The following result extends \autoref{thm3.1new to this setup.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Suppose the joint density of $(X_{1 , \ldots, X_{n )$ is elliptically contoured.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Then, for any $\alpha \in (0,1)$, \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty}FWER_{Bon}(n,\alpha,\Sigma_{n}) = 0\end{equation} under any configuration of true and false null hypotheses, if the following two conditions are satisfied: (i) There exists positive constant $a$ such that $G(a)<1$.
elements.sent-2411-118: The following famous inequality due to \cite{SomeshD will be useful towards proving this.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Suppose the joint density of $(X_{1 , \ldots, X_{k )$ is elliptically contoured.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Let $\mathbf{R =\left(\rho_{i j}\right)$ and $\mathbf{T =\left(\tau_{i j}\right)$ be two correlation matrices.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: If $\rho_{i j \geqslant \tau_{i j}$ holds for all $i, j$, then $l(k, \mathbf{a , \mathbf{R ) \geq l(k, \mathbf{a , \mathbf{T )$, i.e \begin{equation}P_{\Sigma=R}\left[\bigcap_{i=1}^{k}\left\{X_{i} \leqslant a_{i}\right\}\right] \geqslant P_{\Sigma=T}\left[\bigcap_{i=1}^{k}\left\{X_{i} \leqslant a_{i}\right\}\right]\end{equation} holds for all $\mathbf{a =\left(a_{1 , \ldots, a_{k}\right)^{\prime}$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-155: FWER has been a linchpin of simultaneous statistical inference because of its simplicity and relevance across various disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-156: The Bonferroni method, one of the earliest MTPs, has naturally enjoyed widespread use in countless applications.
elements.sent-2411-157: Besides its innumerable applicability, it enjoys interesting theory that often illuminates surprising results and, at the same time, complements applied implications.
elements.sent-2411-158: For example, the asymptotic convexity of the Bonferroni FWER under the equicorrelated Normal setup \citep{das_2021 is a purely probabilistic result in multivariate Normal theory.}
elements.sent-2411-159: However, it also sheds light on the extent of the conservativeness of Bonferroni.
elements.sent-2411-160: A series of works has culminated since then which has tried to sharpen or extend this result in different directions:.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, a unifying theory for a more general class of distributions is nonexistent in the present literature.
elements.sent-2411-162: In a related but different direction, a few other works on the non-asymptotic considerations under the correlated Gaussian sequence model framework \citep{deycstm, deybhandaristpa .}
elements.sent-2411-163: However, the non-asymptotic upper bounds proposed in \cite{deycstm are not asymptotically sharp since they do not converge to zero as the number of hypotheses grows indefinitely.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Thus, a unifying theory for a general class of distributions that encompasses both the asymptotic and non-asymptotic regimes is called for.
elements.sent-2411-165: This paper achieves this by obtaining upper bounds that are asymptotically sharp.
elements.sent-2411-166: We start with an upper bound on the family-wise error rate(FWER) of Bonferroni's procedure for equicorrelated test statistics.
elements.sent-2411-167: We extend this result to general positively correlated elliptically contoured setups and also to the class of step-down procedures.
elements.sent-2411-168: We also present an example of distributions for which Bonferroni FWER has a strictly positive limit under equicorrelation.
elements.sent-2411-169: Lastly, there are possible scopes of interesting extensions in a few other directions.
elements.sent-2411-170: One potential extension is to study the (limiting) behaviors of Hochberg, Hommel, and Benjamini-Hochberg procedures under general distributions.
elements.sent-2411-171: Another natural related question is whether similar asymptotic results hold for other classes of MTPs, e.g., the class of consonant procedures \citep{WTRWH .. Acknowledgements.}
